title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026, approving minutes, acknowledging reports, and making several key decisions including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, directing staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines, and approving construction agreements for multiple road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permit
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Reports
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repair work by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settling over sanitary sewer trenches, and upcoming repairs on Scofield Avenue and Fuller Street for manhole and pavement issues.

Committee members discussed positive feedback from the Kennedy Park Farmers Market's recent opening. Michael noted staff were on-site at 6:00 AM to ensure setup went smoothly. A vendor and residents reported satisfaction with the traffic flow and parking, even with concurrent baseball tournaments.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue lasting approximately one week, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup off East Nick, inlet repairs on Birch, and overhead signing installation on Scofield Avenue. Discussion of flooding issues near Weston Avenue and Zenzer led to consideration of permanent pump solutions.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration in the sanitary system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the water system but 53-54 million gallons treated at wastewater - indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Discussion covered sump pump violations, the upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies, and the need for enforcement.

Michael presented the annual PSC report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure added. The utility has approximately $6.8 million in cash reserves. Acknowledged unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. He noted Wisconsin is affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership but local communities need more state support for recurring road maintenance costs. The committee voted unanimously to recommend adoption.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material, totaling $126,900. He demonstrated this saves the village approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across 2026 projects. Awarded unanimously to King Gravel.

Committee discussed enforcement for residents illegally discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers. Josh and Michael explained the significant cost impact of treating clear water. The committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100/day, distinguishing between unknowing first-time violators (who receive notice to correct) and willful repeat offenders. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 of Radkey Avenue where water/sewer mains don't currently extend. The proposal allows temporary long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, when the property owner will pay assessment costs. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Approved unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for water main relocation and sanitary sewer work is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Approved unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA was selected at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 despite being slightly higher than MSA's $70,400, due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Both approved unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860
Award construction services for Alderson/Jelnik project to MSA and Bladel Avenue project to Clark Dietz
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin concrete repair on Scofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Water main flushing notices to be mailed with flushing starting next week
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact for next meeting
Staff to provide update on street lighting and Scofield Avenue improvements for future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by a commission member, seconded by Jackson, and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole such event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage. The commission approved a $5,000 grant, matching last year's amount, with a request to provide hotel data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously, seconded by Jackson.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates visited local attractions including Mullen's Cheese and expressed appreciation for the Marathon County community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and established a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission taking steps to protect the village's drinking water supply.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 district zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline underneath the parking lot, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity versus current 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed placement and future expansion possibilities to the north. Motion to approve was made by a commissioner and passed unanimously, subject to staff conditions 2-5.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. The program uses a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be approached about compliance. Motion to approve the program passed unanimously, with an amendment to report back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented. Passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps/site plans and building permits were approved unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Commissioner asked about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, and staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project, citing previous concerns about taxpayer costs for excessive landscaping points similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on salt shed inspections within wellhead protection zones rather than letting complaints get 'lost in the sauce.'
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved and recommended to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation within wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar event for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations added to future agenda for fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on introducing department missions, budgets, and key programs to committee members.

agenda.item: Call to order and verification of remote participation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Review of operational functions required by statute
Educational presentations - Review of standing committee ordinances and jurisdiction

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under section 2.04 sub-5, including oversight of the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and relationships with multi-county entities like North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on the new strategic plan development, expected to be completed by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease and promoting community health. She highlighted the department's 37.6 FTEs across four divisions, 1,200 licenses overseen, a new homeless system response position, public health vending machines with free resources including tick kits, and the Nurse Family Partnership program which just graduated its first cohort. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness; Suginaka confirmed robust plans are in place including regular training exercises for measles response.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, covering the department's 140 FTEs and divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, and economic support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program that addresses root causes of housing instability. The department's $22 million budget includes approximately $7 million in tax levy with remainder from state and federal funding.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported the department grew from 2.75 to 3.0 FTEs and had 8,149 client interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits flow to Marathon County veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts after 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional collaboration; Galindo confirmed strong relationships with neighboring counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county regional organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with approximately 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. He described severe funding challenges, noting federal Older Americans Act hasn't been reauthorized in two years while nutrition program costs rose 40-45% in four years, forcing meal service reductions. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135 annually. The ADRC hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

actionItems: North Central Health Care will be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
Chair Boots will bring strategic plan information to the next meeting or the following meeting
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)
Committee members invited to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda items for future meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from committees and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of detailed park maintenance cost data to justify the numbers. Trustee Motti supported the increase, noting population growth requires more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained that Safer fire department partnered with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside to create a reciprocal inspection system for food trucks. Once inspected by any participating department, a sticker is placed on the truck and it doesn't need reinspection in other communities. Ordinance 26-008 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Discussion centered on removing language requiring specific justifications (health, safety, welfare) for remote attendance. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring explanation of reasons for remote attendance, arguing chairs shouldn't have to judge whose excuse is good enough. The amendment passed with two no votes from Hermling and Motti. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope Sorrell, approved unanimously. Parks committee, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future board packets.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue location. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained the history: the original site had 25 stalls, Camp Phillips had 42 stalls but no vehicle parking for vendors, and Kennedy Park has 53 spots with vendor vehicle parking. Trustee Motti noted Mont Miller Park was considered but rejected as too far from traffic. Discussion included potential impacts from the upcoming roundabout construction on Alderson. No action was taken; this was discussion only.

Director of Public Works Michael Dolan addressed public comment concerns about the unfilled engineering technician position, explaining it requires a very specific skill set that is scarce in central Wisconsin. Even consulting firms are struggling to find local talent. The board approved contracts for outside engineering consultants for two projects starting in June (items 68-69) as interim measures.

The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained the license is required under state statute for businesses buying and selling precious metals to protect against stolen merchandise. Motion by Hermling, second by Clark, amended to require satisfactory background check and receipt of occupancy permit since the location is still under construction. Approved unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the village for spending an estimated $108,000 on outside engineering consultants while the engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why the position hasn't been filled and called for administrator accountability. Pitel also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting that Lisa Beck was introduced at the April Fire Department meeting as a Safer board member before being officially appointed. He called for more transparency and public discussion of appointments. Additionally, Pitel expressed disappointment that no detailed Kennedy Park information was included in the packet despite previous requests. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel also questioned the lack of detailed operational cost data to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance (26-008) adopted
E-bike regulations ordinance (26-012) adopted
Nuisance ordinance (26-011) adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding adopted
Comprehensive Plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6 PM
Staff to include committee/commission applicant resumes in future board packets
Staff to bring back Weston Avenue speed limit study results to joint meeting
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following severe weather that impacted the Ringle area. The board suspended its rules to address the emergency tipping fee modification, which will reduce fees from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified storm-related debris through June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and silent moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing supervisor leadership skills, and honoring supervisor preferences. Over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she had seen her advocate for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Director Dave Hagen Busher presented the resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board regarding reduced tipping fees for disaster debris following severe weather in Ringle. The state waived $13.10/ton, Ringle waived $2.33/ton, and the county proposed reducing fees from $65/ton to $36.94/ton (covering costs only). Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely and extend the timeline beyond 60 days. After extensive discussion about insurance coverage delays, the 60-day timeline, and future policy development, the board unanimously voted to suspend rules and then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive its fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle have waived their fees, and that private businesses have donated over $40,000 in cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting that some of the 44 affected homes still await insurance adjusters and requesting more time and fee relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved (out of home placement reserve increase) - not unanimous
Resolution 25-26 approved (social services budget amendment) - not unanimous
Resolution 26-26 approved unanimously - reduced landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Board to revisit tipping fee reduction timeline at May meeting if extension needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-impacted properties to the county board. The committee also received educational presentations from three county departments: Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Introduction to departments appearing before committee
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition - Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained there is currently a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make the non-conforming home use conforming. The Town Board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Consideration of fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties impacted by the April 17 tornado affecting the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future disasters. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to others not in declared disaster areas. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160 each) for pouts systems, excluding other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor K and carried. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher provided an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill history since 1980, the new renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. He noted the department has 11 FTE staff processing up to 2,000 tons daily.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's operation of 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and services to the City of Wausau. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced that Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as the new county forest administrator.

actionItems: Forward rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley) to County Board for approval
Forward rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan) to County Board for approval

Forward amended resolution to County Board waiving only pouts reconnect fees ($160) for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather, with eligibility based on emergency management damage assessment or self-certification
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved kayak launch signage for the Plover River Water Trail, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course. Public commenters expressed opposition to the dog day proposal and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion was held.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan development, noting the comprehensive plan was approved in November 2023 and the master plan in December 2023. The total project cost is approximately $14.3 million with the village responsible for roughly half. The Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for field lights, having approximately $84-85,000 of the $117,000 goal at the community foundation. Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital dollars, with Oster Bring confirming no village funds are currently earmarked pending project development. A public check presentation is planned for the first tournament in June.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and documentation of tree removal from the Plover River and installation of protective wood mats over gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan showed the location of removed trees that were blocking the river and explained the mat installation process over the TC Energy (formerly ANR/ATC) pipelines. The pipeline company representative was present during the work and conducted a walkthrough afterward. The village has extra mats that may be used for future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Chair Katrina, who had requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. Public commenter Andy from 6504 [street name unclear] stated he believes 'this is ridiculous' and 'there should not be animals allowed into a public pool.' His objection was noted for the record.

Director Oster Bring explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting their best tool (a sandblaster on a pressure washer) cannot access the trail due to narrow bridges and steep ditches. Memorial benches weigh 2,000 pounds each. Committee members discussed anti-vandalism coatings and seal coats as potential solutions. Laura suggested volunteer work days, noting she would participate and believes trail users would help. Staff will explore cleaning options and potentially address items during off-season or one at a time.

Dan Nitz from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version seven of signage for the Plover River Water Trail. Signs will be installed at launches #3 (Highway J), #4 (Ross), and #5 (Yellow Banks) in Weston, plus locations in Schofield. Marathon County Parks Foundation is funding the signs through state waterway grants. The signs include QR codes linking to the Kelly Station river gauge and donation URL to the foundation. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve the signs for installation passed unanimously.

Staff mentioned receiving a request about food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds, stating one in 13 people have food allergies. The signs would discourage eating on play equipment. Committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion. Director Oster Bring also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin, with a kickoff meeting with Rettler planned for the following Tuesday.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing the dog day at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool' and suggesting dogs belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Street, Weston), speaking via Zoom under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned the Kennedy Park funding report showing exactly $8,700 in receipts and expenditures as 'too coincidental,' asked about the location of a $10,000 grant and Glow Games proceeds, and called the numbers 'convoluted and misleading.' He questioned why the Friends of Kennedy Park won't share detailed financials.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June 2026 meeting
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J launches
Staff to explore cleaning solutions and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail benches and concrete
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future committee discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for following Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from the Municipal Clerk's office covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. Committee member Evan Soaper announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with no discussion.

Discussion focused on TIF closure timeline. Staff indicated they are anticipating October-November for final bond payoff, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds this summer, after which Ehlers will send notification to US Bank and DTC. Staff confirmed final numbers look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of the clerk's office operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. The office has moved from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time employee. Notable discussion included postage costs increasing from $6,000 to $15,000 for fall elections, pet licensing fees doubling from $10 to $20 due to county requirements, and the non-dog stray contract costing $175 per animal including mice. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing. Chief election inspector pay may be increased from $13 to $15 per hour in the next term.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been present for the last three Saturdays from 6 AM for 6-7 hours. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors will increase mid to end of June. Concern was raised about parking on both sides of Alderson Street, with clarification that only the west side allows parking now.

Evan Soaper announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though still in the metro area. This was his last meeting. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA resolution to call TIF bonds expected this summer
Bond payoff anticipated October-November 2026
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th to address multiple ordinance amendments and operational matters. Key actions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure on Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
E-bike ordinance in Chapter 82 traffic and vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street between Mount Olive Church and DC Everest
Mobile food vendors license fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location, expressing concerns that parking vehicles on grass would destroy the park and potentially violate village code. He questioned ADA-compliant handicap parking placement on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the origin of new fire inspections for food vendors, and criticized the age of pending code enforcement cases, noting some have been open for years without visible progress.

Roman/Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week. Two SROs are assigned to zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee discussed final amendments including tree limb heights over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet), with language added requiring compliance with the jurisdiction having authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on emotos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee discussed whether to include electric scooters, with Trustee Tolson noting many middle schoolers use them. Chief Hunt suggested adding 'with exemption to kids play scooters' for officer discretion. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with emotos prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking window (4am-4pm) is far outside what surrounding municipalities use (typically 5-hour windows from 2am-7am). Michael from public works noted residential streets aren't plowed until 7-8am anyway. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions for enforcement purposes, noting citations were reduced to $10 to change behavior. Committee tabled the item, directing Michael to identify 6-8 problem areas for specific signage, to be revisited in July/August.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school property. Committee debated whether to install a basic crosswalk ($2-3K) or lighted system ($25-30K). Chief Hunt reported no incidents in the area. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously. Michael will contact them.

Chief Hunt explained a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer. Any participating department can inspect food trucks; a sticker indicates compliance valid across all jurisdictions. Wausau opted out to maintain their own program. Chief noted one recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt identified an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and was previously considered for a fire training burn but was too deteriorated. Previous owners had housed chickens inside. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village handle demolition with costs added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items including concerns about the new farmers market location damaging park grass, improper ADA parking placement on grass, questioning the origin of food truck fire inspections, ambiguity in food vendor ordinance language, and criticism of long-pending code enforcement cases without visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
Michael to identify 6-8 problem parking areas for specific signage; overnight parking regulations to return July/August
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if they agree to pay, recommend board approval
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections for Delicoski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue to Board of Trustees
Investigate Weston Villa Apartments addressing issue on Delicoski Street
Recommend raze order authorization for 4104 Shore Avenue to Board of Trustees
Investigate property with furniture and dumpster at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

agenda.item: Elect Chairperson and Vice Chairperson for the 2026-2028 term
Establish regular meeting date and time for the 2026-2028 term
Consideration of April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee Minutes
Approving Consent to Transfer, Waiver of Right of First Refusal, Termination of Deed Restrictions and Approval of New Deed Restrictions for 725 S. 84th Avenue

discussions.body: The committee held elections for leadership positions for the new 2026-2028 term. Specific vote counts and election results are not recorded in the official vote records.

discussions.body: The committee established its regular meeting schedule for the 2026-2028 term. The specific date and time selected and any vote count are not recorded in the official vote records.
The committee considered the minutes from the April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee meeting. The outcome of any approval vote is not recorded in the official vote records.

discussions.item: Approving Consent to Transfer, Waiver of Right of First Refusal, Termination of Deed Restrictions and Approval of New Deed Restrictions for 725 S. 84th Avenue

The committee received a discussion item providing an overview of the Community Development Department. This was an informational item with no action required.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to consider action on a pupil expulsion matter during or following the closed session hearing

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects

Mayoral appointments confirmed to Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board
Amendments to Ordinance 02-0432 as stated by Alder Watson and Alder Tierney to be reviewed by City Attorney
